title: Electronic and Photonic Integration of Single Quantum Emitters in 2D Materials
authors: Sahil D. Patel, Sean Doan, Luka Jevremovic, Kamyar Parto, Galan Moody
abstract: Single-photon sources that are bright, pure, and interference-ready are essential for quantum communication and photonic quantum information processing, but many solid-state platforms still rely on bulky optical excitation, careful alignment, and post-selection to achieve useful linewidth, stability, and brightness. Scalable quantum photonics instead requires turnkey quantum-light engines that can be triggered on demand, stabilized against environmental noise, and efficiently interfaced with fibers or photonic circuits. This review surveys recent progress in electronic and photonic integration of single quantum emitters in two-dimensional materials, focusing on localized excitonic emitters in transition metal dichalcogenides and defect-based color centers in hexagonal boron nitride. On the electronic side, we discuss electrical injection, fast modulation, electrostatic stabilization, and Stark tunability as routes to suppress blinking, spectral wandering, and charge-noise-induced broadening. On the photonic side, we review waveguide and resonator platforms that funnel emission into well-defined optical modes and, in some cases, enhance radiative rates through the Purcell effect. We connect these integration strategies to key source metrics, including single-photon purity, brightness, spectral stability, and photon indistinguishability. We conclude that the next stage of progress will depend on co-designed electronic and photonic architectures that jointly optimize on-demand operation, stabilization, tunability, and packaging-compatible optical interfacing.
summary: This review examines how integrating electronic and photonic components into 2D materials can transform single quantum emitters into scalable, 'turnkey' quantum light sources. It highlights how electrical control can stabilize spectral noise while photonic structures can maximize photon collection. The work emphasizes that co-designing these two domains is essential for practical quantum information processing.
main_problem: Current solid-state single-photon sources rely on bulky, non-scalable laboratory setups and suffer from spectral instability and low collection efficiency, preventing their use in large-scale quantum photonic networks.
main_result: The paper argues that the path to scalable quantum light engines lies in the intentional co-design of electronic integration (to stabilize and tune emitters) and photonic integration (to enhance brightness and extraction efficiency).
method: The authors review recent progress in using electrostatic gating, electrical injection, and strain engineering for electronic control, alongside waveguide and resonator integration for photonic mode engineering in 2D materials.
model_or_system: The review focuses on single quantum emitters in two-dimensional materials, specifically localized excitons in transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) like WSe2 and defect-based color centers in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN).
key_observables: Single-photon purity (g2(0)), brightness, photon indistinguishability, spectral linewidth, radiative lifetime (T1), coherence time (T2), and Purcell enhancement.
important_parameters: Spectral wandering, charge-noise-induced broadening, Stark tuning ranges, and extraction efficiency.
main_assumptions: The review assumes that the transition from stochastic, randomly generated emitters to designed, deterministic sites is the necessary next step for scalability.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the impact of charge noise and the benefits of gated architectures and photonic confinement; Figure 2 shows the spectral diversity of hBN defects; Figure 3 details various electrical injection architectures like vertical and lateral junctions; Figure 11 shows proposed integrated electronic-photonic stacks.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the scalability bottleneck, then reviews electronic integration strategies (injection, gating, tuning), followed by photonic integration (waveguides, resonators), and concludes by proposing a co-design framework for integrated architectures.
why_interesting: It addresses the fundamental engineering of light-matter interfaces and the control of decoherence (charge noise) in solid-state systems, which is critical for developing scalable quantum networks and cavity QED-like architectures in 2D materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electronic stabilization and photonic enhancement of 2D quantum emitters. (a) An input excitation train—delivered either optically or electrically—consists of pulses with temporal width tp and repetition period td. Excitation is converted into an emissive state with quantum efficiency Qe and coupled to an on-chip photonic cavity, where the radiative lifetime T1 sets the characteristic emission timescale. The generated photons are extracted and routed with an overall collection efficiency η, yielding an output field whose temporal coherence is captured by the coherence time T2 (envelope ∝e−t/T2). (b) A defect-based quantum emitter (red) embedded in a solid- state host experiences...

FIG. 2. Spectral diversity of quantum emitters in hBN across ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared wavelength bands. (a) Representative ultraviolet photoluminescence spectra from multiple sites, illustrating site-to-site variability in UV emission features (adapted from [1] under the CC BY 4.0 license). (b) Visible-range emission spectra from distinct emitters, plotted and labeled by their zero-phonon line (ZPL) wavelength, highlighting a broad distribution of discrete ZPL positions across the visible band (adapted from [2], Copyright 2025, American Chemical Society). (c) Histogram of NIR emitter ZPL wavelengths measured under two excitation conditions (660 nm and 765 nm) at cryogenic...

FIG. 3. Electrical Injection Theory and Device Concepts. (a) Schematic of a representative vertical vdW heterostructure used for electrical injection into TMD emitters. A monolayer WSe2 contacted by top and bottom graphene electrodes, with a thin hBN spacer serving as a tunneling barrier to enable bias-controlled carrier injection and electroluminescence from the TMD layer (adapted from [14] under the CC BY 4.0 license). FIG. 4. Electrical injection of quantum emitters in atomically-thin TMD–based QLEDs. (a) Vertical graphene/hBN/WSe2 tunnel-junction LED device used to electrically drive emission in monolayer WSe2 (adapted from [55] under the CC BY 4.0 license).
